title: Is AI Actually Fair?

modules.title: The Hiring Algorithm Nobody Questioned
modules.sub: Discover how an unchallenged AI caused real harm
modules.description: Learners examine a real-world case where an AI system made consequential decisions without anyone stopping to ask if it was fair. They identify the warning signs that were missed and begin to understand why questioning AI systems matters. This sets the stage for their own investigation into the school tutoring algorithm.

modules.title: Where Does Bias Come From
modules.sub: Trace the hidden origins of unfairness in AI systems
modules.description: Learners explore the different ways bias can enter an AI system, from the people who design it to the goals they set and the shortcuts they take. They sort real examples into categories of bias and debate which causes are hardest to spot. By the end, they can name at least three distinct sources of bias and explain how each one damages outcomes.

modules.title: Data as a Mirror of the Past
modules.sub: See how historical inequality gets baked into training data
modules.description: Learners investigate how the data used to train AI systems often reflects past discrimination rather than a fair picture of the world. They analyse a sample dataset representing student performance and spot patterns that could lead an AI to make biased predictions. They then discuss why simply using more data does not automatically fix the problem.

modules.title: Testing an AI for Fairness
modules.sub: Run structured checks on the school tutoring algorithm
modules.description: Learners apply practical fairness-testing techniques to the scenario's tutoring AI, comparing its recommendations across different student groups. They use a step-by-step audit framework to document what they find and flag specific patterns that look discriminatory. This hands-on investigation builds the evidence base they will need for their final report.

modules.title: When Fair for Some Means Unfair for Others
modules.sub: Wrestle with competing definitions of fairness that cannot all coexist
modules.description: Learners discover that fairness is not a single agreed standard — mathematically, you often cannot satisfy every definition at the same time. They work through a dilemma in which making the AI fairer for one group creates a disadvantage for another, and must decide which trade-off they can justify. This module pushes them to think about values, not just formulas.

modules.title: Your Fairness Audit Report
modules.sub: Write a real recommendation to keep, fix, or scrap the system
modules.description: Learners bring together all their findings into a structured written audit report addressed to the fictional school leadership team. They must clearly state whether the AI should be kept as-is, redesigned, or discontinued, and back their recommendation with evidence gathered across the course. The completed report is their assessed artefact and demonstrates mastery of the course learning outcome.

synopsis: A new AI system is being used to decide which students get extra tutoring resources at your school â and the patterns are troubling. You must investigate, challenge assumptions, and ultimately recommend whether the system should be kept, fixed, or scrapped.
rationale: Young people aged 14â16 are beginning to encounter AI-driven decisions in education, employment, and social platforms, but few have the frameworks to identify or challenge bias embedded in systems that appear objective.
learning_outcome: Students can conduct a structured audit of an AI system for potential bias, identify the source of that bias in data or design, and produce a written recommendation for how the system should be changed or governed.
